segments.s251-g65: کشیدیم is past-tense first-person-plural ‘we endured/suffered’ (کشیدن used here in the sense of suffering). و is the simple conjunction 'and'. گشتیم is past-tense plural ‘we became’ (گشتن = to become). خشنود is an adjective 'pleased, content' and stands after گشتیم as its complement: 'we became pleased'. ازوی is the prepositional/relational phrase 'از وی' = 'because of / with / from him', marking the cause or object of the feeling.
segments.s251-g66: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'because of' marking cause. کینه دل من is a genitive phrase 'the hatred of my heart' (دل من = 'my heart'). بیاسود is a predicative adjective/verb form meaning 'did not find rest / was not comforted' (here describing the state of the heart). ازوی again means 'because of him' and repeats the causal agent.
segments.s251-g67: فروهشت is a past tense/archaic poetic verb meaning 'scattered' or 'cast down' (here used with رستم as the agent). The word order places the verb before the subject (classical/poetic order): 'فروهشت رستم' = 'Rostam scattered/cast'. بزندان is the prefixed adverbial 'to/into the prison' (ب + زندان) marking direction/place. کمند is the object 'snare/lasso' that was cast.
segments.s251-g68: برآوردش is برآورد + ش (the enclitic object 'him') and here functions as a past action 'he brought/drew him out' (poetic usage of برآورد for bringing out). از is the preposition 'from/out of'. چاه is 'well' (place). با is 'with/by means of'. پای‌بند is literal 'foot-bound' (here 'with his feet tied'), an adjective/phrase describing how he was brought out.
segments.s251-g69: برهنه is the adjective 'naked/unclothed' modifying the immediately following noun تن ('body'). The coordination 'و موی و ناخن' lists 'and hair and nails'; دراز ('long') postmodifies the closest coordinated items (here understood to describe hair and nails as 'long'). The conjunctions و simply join the nouns.
segments.s251-g70: گدازیده is a participial/adjective meaning 'melted' or figuratively 'consumed' (by emotion/suffering) and modifies the subject phrase. از introduces the cause: 'from/out of'. رنج, درد, نیاز are a three-part coordination giving the reasons: 'suffering, pain, and longing/need' — here 'نیاز' reads best as 'longing' or desperate need.
segments.s251-g71: همه تن is a nominal phrase 'the whole body' (همه = entire). پر از uses the adjective 'پر' with the preposition از to mean 'full of' (پر از خون = 'full of blood'). و links the next phrase رخساره زرد where رخساره refers to face/complexion and زرد here means 'pale/wan' rather than simply 'yellow'.
segments.s251-g72: ازان is a compound demonstrative/prepositional 'from that' referring back to the fetter; بند here means 'fetter/bond' (a shackle). زنجیر is 'chain' and زنگار is 'rust/tarnish'. خورد is the past tense verb often used idiomatically with damage/decay (زنگار خوردن) meaning 'was eaten/affected by rust' or 'became rusty' — so the clause says the chain of that fetter had become rusted.
segments.s251-g1: بدانگه is a temporal adverb meaning 'at that moment/thereupon.' که introduces the subordinate clause ('when/that'), followed by the subject رستم. ببربر + گره is an attributive noun phrase: ببربر qualifies گره (the head noun), so the phrase means 'the Berber's knot' or 'the knot of the barbar(ian)'; classical Persian often stacks modifiers before the noun without an explicit ezāfe.
segments.s251-g2: برافگند is a perfect/past-form verb here meaning 'he loosed/threw off' (the action carried out by the already-mentioned subject Rostam). و links the two actions; زد is the second past verb 'he struck'. The prepositional phrases follow زد: زد بر گره = 'struck at/on the knot' and بر زره = 'upon the armor' (two separate بر phrases specifying target and surface).
segments.s251-g3: بشد is a classical third-person form meaning 'it happened/it became' or 'came to be'; here it introduces the appearance/coming into presence. پیش یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of God' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', یزدان = 'God'), and خورشید و ماه (sun and moon) are the entities that appear — note the verb-initial classical order.
segments.s251-g4: بیامد is a past verb 'came' and بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (بدو = به+او). کرد is used here in the sense 'made/caused' and takes پشت و پناه as its object phrase: پشت و پناه = 'back and refuge' idiomatically 'support and protection' — together meaning 'they came to him and made (themselves) his support and refuge.'
segments.s251-g5: همی before گفت gives a continuous or iterative sense ('kept saying' / continuous action). The clause چشم بدان کور باد is an optative/curse: چشم = 'eye', بدان (here written as ب + دان) functions deictically as 'that'/'to that', کور = 'blind', and باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (a wish/curse). Word order is emphatic: verb phrase (گفت) then the wish clause.
segments.s251-g6: بدین کار is a prepositional phrase 'by/for this deed/affair' (here split as ب + دین + کار in the segmentation; together = 'in/for this matter'). بیژن is the proper name 'Bijan'. مرا را (segmented as م + را) functions as the object 'to me' or 'for me' (مرا = 'me'); زور = 'strength', and باد is again the optative 'may it be' — overall: 'May Bijan be strength to me in this matter.'
segments.s251-g7: بگردان here is an imperative or a verbal form meaning 'turn! / cause to turn / (in some contexts) call/command' — in context it functions like a verbal order or the verb used in his command. بفرمود is an honorific past 'he commanded/said (politely)'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that' and همینگونه (همچنین) = 'thus/likewise' indicates manner.
segments.s251-g8: ببستند is a third-person plural past 'they bound/tied'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon', گردگه is the location noun (here 'place/spot' or possibly 'neck/round' in idiomatic sense), بند is 'fetter/bond' (the thing tied), and کین is a genitival qualifier 'of hatred/vengeance' — 'they tied/bound upon the place a bond/fetter of hatred.' The agent 'they' is implicit in the verb.
segments.s251-g81: ازان پس is a temporal phrase meaning 'after that' (ازان = 'from that' + پس = 'after'). چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning 'when'. گرگین is the proper name Gorgin. بنزدیک is an adverbial/locative meaning 'near' or 'close by', and اوی is an archaic third‑person pronoun 'him' (object), so the whole chunk means 'after that, when Gorgin came close to him.'
segments.s251-g82: بیامد is past 3rd‑person 'came' (he came forward). بمالید is past 3rd of مالیدن 'rubbed' (sequence of actions: he came and then rubbed). بر governs the following noun phrase, and خاک روی is 'the surface of the earth / the ground' (روی here = 'surface'), so بمالید بر خاک روی = 'rubbed (his) face on the ground.'
segments.s251-g83: ز here is the poetic ablative 'because of / from' and combines with کردار بد 'bad deed / bad conduct'. پوزش آورد is a common phrase 'brought/uttered an apology' (آورد past 3rd). پیش functions as 'before' or 'in the presence of' (i.e., he apologized before the other person).
segments.s251-g84: بپیچید is past 3rd of پیچیدن, here meaning 'he turned (away)/withdrew' (used of retracting or turning aside). زان = 'from that' (referring to the foregoing), خام qualifies کردار as 'raw / ill‑considered / rash', and خویش + ش (enclitic ـش) is the possessive 'his own', so the phrase means 'he turned away from that rash deed of his.'
segments.s251-g85: دل بیژن is 'Bijan's heart' (possessive noun phrase). از کینش gives the cause 'from / because of his hatred (vengeance)'. آمد براه is an idiomatic combination: آمد (literally 'came') + براه ('on the road / toward action') meaning 'his heart took to the road' — idiomatically 'he resolved to go' or 'he was moved to set out.'
segments.s251-g86: مکافات is 'retribution / punishment' (object). ناورد is a past form meaning literally 'did not bring', but here idiomatically indicates that he did not fail to carry out retribution (i.e., he exacted punishment). پیش گناه places that retribution 'with respect to / for the offense' (پیش in this archaic order = 'regarding / before' the fault).
segments.s251-g87: شتر بار کردند is a simple past plural clause 'they loaded the camels' (کردند = 'they did'). The conjunction و links the next phrase; اسبان بزین is elliptical coordination meaning 'and (they) saddled the horses' (بزین here functions as the verb 'to saddle / equip'), with the verb understood from the first clause.
segments.s251-g88: بپوشید is past 3rd 'he put on / donned' (Rostam is the subject). سلیح گزین is a noun + postpositive modifier: سلیح 'weapon/arms' and گزین 'chosen/selected' (here adjectival/participle), so 'Rostam put on his chosen arms.'
segments.s251-g89: نشستند is the 3rd-person-plural past of نشستن (here 'they sat' / 'they took their places') and appears before the following prepositional phrase in the poetic word order. بر باره ناموران is a prepositional/group phrase: بر 'on/upon', باره here 'occasion/instance' (i.e. the place/charge), and ناموران 'the renowned' (plural); in poetry the ezāfe/genitive link is implicit, so the phrase means roughly 'took their place upon/at the (field) of the famed.'
segments.s251-g90: کشیدند is the 3rd-person-plural past of کشیدن used with the weapon sense 'they drew' (i.e. drew weapons). The direct objects follow: شمشیر 'sword' and گرز 'mace/club', with the adjective گران 'heavy' modifying گرز (post‑nominal adjective), a normal order in Persian poetry.
segments.s251-g91: گسی is an indefinite pronoun 'someone' or 'a certain one'; کرد is the simple past 'did/made' (3rd-sing or 3rd-plural depending on context) taking بار 'the load/baggage' as its object. The conjunction و links to برآراست کار: برآراست is a past-tense verb meaning 'arranged/put in order' and کار 'affairs/work' is its object—so the clause means 'someone loaded the baggage and arranged the affairs.'
segments.s251-g92: چنانچون بود is a comparative/formulaic phrase 'just as/just so it was'; در خور is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'appropriate to/fit for' and کارزار is 'battle(bfield)'. Together the clause reads 'as was fitting for the battlefield.' Note خور here functions adjectivally in the idiom در خور, an older usage not listed in the provided senses.
segments.s251-g93: بشد is a past/optative form of شدن here meaning 'he rose/ he set out' (3rd-singular narrative). با introduces the accompanying item/person: بنه 'a bundle/pack' with the noun اشکش (here functioning as a proper name, genitive 'Ashkash's') and the epithet تیزهوش 'quick‑witted' follows as a post‑nominal adjective. In classical poetry the ezāfe is often omitted, so بنه اشکش reads as 'the pack of Ashkash.'
segments.s251-g94: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'; دارد is the present (habitual) 'has/keeps'; سپه را uses را as the definite object marker 'the army'; بهرجای is an adverbial 'everywhere/to each place'; گوش here is used figuratively as 'attention/ear' (i.e. 'keeps the army's ear/attention everywhere'). The clause characterizes the preceding quick‑witted companion.
segments.s251-g95: به بیژن 'to Bijan' (به + proper name) is the indirect object/recipient; بفرمود is an honorific/simple past 'he commanded/said' (here referring to Rostam). رستم names the speaker/agent (appearing after the verb, a common classical word order). که introduces the content clause and شو is an imperative/subjunctive 'be/ become' used here to mark the command addressed to Bijan.
segments.s251-g96: تو 'you' (emphatic vocative/subject) begins the direct command. با ... و با ... uses با 'with' twice to mark two companions: اشکش (here a proper name; the provided senses list gives non‑matching meanings, so treated as a name) and منیژه 'Manijeh' (proper name). برو is the 2nd‑person imperative 'go.' The structure is a plain imperative: 'You, go with Ashkash and with Manijeh.'
segments.s251-g97: This is the clause-initial reason phrase introduced by که used as 'because'. ما is the 1st-person plural subject ('we'), امشب is a temporal adverb ('tonight'), and از + کین افراسیاب is a prepositional phrase giving the cause ('because of Afrasiab's hatred'). Word order places the subject and time before the causal phrase, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s251-g98: نیابیم is a negative present/subjunctive 1st‑person plural verb form meaning 'we do not / we shall not obtain/find' (the negative prefix ن- plus یابیم). آرام, خورد and خواب are coordinated nouns ('rest/peace', 'eating/food', 'sleep') that are all within the scope of the negation; the connective و links the items.
segments.s251-g99: یکی کار ('a certain deed/one plan') is the direct object phrase; سازم is the 1st‑person singular verb 'I make/prepare' with the clitic ending -م marking the subject. کنون is the adverb 'now'. بر درِ ش (here written as بر درش) is a locative prepositional phrase 'at/on his door(gate)', with ش the possessive enclitic 'his'. The verb precedes some modifiers, a common ordering in older Persian.
segments.s251-g100: که introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that' / 'in order that'). فردا is the temporal adverb 'tomorrow'. بخندد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive/optative form 'may he laugh' / 'he will laugh'. The two verbs بخندد and برو are coordinated, giving a sequence 'he will laugh and go'; برو here means 'go' and کشورِ ش is 'his country' with the possessive enclitic.
segments.s251-g101: بدو گفت is an inversion meaning 'to him he said' (بدو = 'to him'); گفت is the speech verb 'said' (note: the provided sense list did not include the simple past verb sense). بیژن is the speaker's name. منم is the emphatic copula 'I am' (من + -م), and پیش‌رو is a predicate noun meaning 'foremost/forerunner' or 'vanguard'.
segments.s251-g102: که introduces the explanatory/reason clause ('for' / 'because'). از من marks the source/target of the action ('from me' / 'against me'), همی is a classical verbal particle giving habitual/affirmative force to the following verb, کینه is 'hatred, enmity', سازند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive 'they make' (they will cause), and نو functions as 'new'/'anew' (i.e., 'they will make enmity anew').
segments.s251-g73: خروشید is a past-tense verb ('he roared/uttered a cry') placed before the subject رستم for poetic inversion (verb–subject order). چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause here meaning 'when'; او را uses او + the definite-object marker را to mark 'him' as the object of بدید, where بدید is the simple past 'saw.'
segments.s251-g74: همه تن = 'the whole body' (همه modifying تن). در آهن شده is a predicate using شده (past participle/become) with در + noun to indicate 'become into/turned to iron' in a poetic phrasing, and ناپدید is a predicate adjective meaning 'vanished' or 'became unseen.' The subject is contextually the person just seen; the verb شُده governs ناپدید as a subsequent state.
segments.s251-g75: بزد is the past-tense verb 'he struck/struck out.' دست here names the instrument ('with his hand' / simply 'hand'), and the two verbs are linked by و. بگسست is an intransitive/pseudo-passive past 'broke apart' (i.e., the chain was broken). زنجیر و بند are the coordinated nouns receiving that action ('chain and bond/fetter').
segments.s251-g76: رها کرد is the compound past 'released' (رها + کردن); کرد is the light verb carrying tense. ازو = از + او 'from him/it' (archaic contraction) marks source. حلقه پای بند is a noun phrase 'the ring of the foot-fetter' (حلقه = ring/loop, پای‌بند = foot-fetter/shackle), i.e. the shackle-loop removed from his foot.
segments.s251-g77: سوی خانه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the house' placed before the verb for emphasis; رفتند is past plural 'they went.' زان is an archaic/contractional form of از + آن 'from that,' and چاهسار is the place noun 'well-field' (the locality they leave).
segments.s251-g78: This nominal fragment describes the people accompanying the motion already expressed by رفتند. بیک دست is likely a poetic/archaic manner phrase 'with one hand' (instrumental), followed by the name بیژن in apposition. بدیگر here means 'and the others' / 'to the others,' and زوار describes them as worn/rumpled/tatters. The main verb is omitted (elliptical) because it refers back to رفتند above.
segments.s251-g79: تهمتن is the proper name (epithet of Rostam). بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/ordered' (third person). شستن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'the washing (of)' used as the object of the command, and سرش = سر + ـش 'his head' is the direct object of that verbal noun.
segments.s251-g80: یکی here functions as 'one/a certain (person)' or simply 'someone.' جامه پوشید is the verb phrase 'put on a garment' (جامه = garment, پوشید = past 'put on'). نو = 'new' modifies جامه; بر is the preposition 'on/upon.' برش appears here as a contracted sequence (preposition + clitic) meaning 'on him' in context, but the headlemma senses listed do not reflect that clitic use.
segments.s251-g25: ز + رخش + اندر آمد is a short poetic clause: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' (marking source/starting point), رخش 'Rakhsh' is the steed, and اندر آمد is a simple past ('came in/forth'). گو is an archaic/elliptical particle here used like 'as if' or 'as though' (a poetic simile) and modifies شیرنر ('like a lion'). Word order is source → location/verb → simile.
segments.s251-g26: This is a V-final Persian clause with the object before the verb: 'زره دامنش را' is the definite object ('the hem of his armor') marked by را, بزد is 3rd-person singular past 'he struck' (or 'he beat'), and 'بر کمر' is a locative phrase ('to/around his waist') with بر. Here دامنش is the possessed noun (دامن + ـش).
segments.s251-g27: ز یزدان = 'from God' (ز as 'from'); یزدان 'God' is followed by the epithet جان‌آفرین 'life-giving' in apposition. زور خواست is a past tense clause: زور (strength) is the thing asked for and خواست is the 3rd-person past of خواستن 'to want/ask for' (i.e. 'sought/asked'). The order is prepositional phrase → noun phrase epithet → object → past verb.
segments.s251-g28: This is a sequence of two coordinated past actions. بزد دست literally 'he struck (with) his hand'—بزد is past 3sg and دست is the instrumental/complement. و links the clauses. آن سنگ برداشت is 'he lifted that stone' (آن = 'that', سنگ = 'stone', برداشت = past 'took up/raised'). داست is a noun placed after برداشت (poetic apposition) and here functions metaphorically (see senses).
segments.s251-g29: بینداخت is past 'he threw' (simple past). در is the preposition 'into/in'. بیشه 'grove, thicket' is the place; شهر چین is a compound genitival phrase 'the city of Chin' (شهر 'city' + چین 'Chin/China'). Word order: verb → prepositional phrase → place-name.
segments.s251-g30: بلرزید is 3rd-person past 'it/he trembled/quaked'. ازان is the older/dialectal form of 'از آن' ('from that' / 'because of that'), linking cause to effect. سنگ is the cause 'that stone', and روی زمین is a locative phrase 'on/upon the ground' (روی used as preposition 'on').
segments.s251-g31: ز بیژن is a prepositional/agent-like phrase 'of/from Bijan' (here marking the person addressed or the source of information). بپرسید is past third‑person 'he asked' (classical past form with the prefix ب- preserved in epic diction). و links the verb to نالید 'moaned/wailed', and زار modifies نالید as an adverb 'bitterly/wretchedly'.
segments.s251-g32: که introduces the content clause ('that/asking how ...'); چون is the interrogative/adverb 'how' here. بود is the past copula 'was', کارت 'your affair/condition' is the topic, ببد is an archaic variant of the copula meaning 'was'/'became' in epic language, and روزگار 'fate/time' gives the sense 'ill fate/fortune'. The clause roughly asks 'how was your case by bad fortune?' with archaic copula usage.
segments.s251-g9: بر اسبان is a prepositional phrase ('on the horses') with بر before the plural noun اسبان; نهادند is the past, 3rd-person plural of نهادن 'to place/put'. زین خدنگ is the direct object placed after the verb (poetic compression: two nouns juxtaposed to mean 'saddles and daggers'). The overall order shows a classical Persian, slightly inverted, S(implicit) – P(Prep phrase) – V – O pattern.
segments.s251-g10: همه جنگ را is a definite object phrase ('the whole of the war') marked by را; تیز کردند is the past, 3rd-person plural ('they made sharp' / 'they sharpened'). The noun چنگ follows the verb and functions here as the instrument/imagery being sharpened ('claw' used metaphorically for weapons). The line thus means they honed everything for battle.
segments.s251-g11: تهمتن is the subject (a heroic epithet); برخشنده is a postpositive adjective/epithet meaning 'radiant' modifying تهمتن; بنهاد is the past 3rd-person singular of نهادن 'he set/placed' and here combines with روی ('face') to form the idiom 'روی بنهاد' = 'he turned/set his face (toward)'.
segments.s251-g12: همی رفت is a frequent epic construction: همی with the past verb رفت gives an ongoing/progressive past sense ('he proceeded/was going on'); پیش functions adverbially 'forward'; اندرون راه جوی is a genitive chain 'the inner path of the stream' (اندرون modifying راه, which is further specified by جوی).
segments.s251-g13: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; آمد is past 'came'; بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; سنگ is the noun 'stone/rock'; اکوان فراز is a compressed descriptive phrase 'of the worlds, on high' — فراز is a postpositive element meaning 'on high/above', so the phrase reads 'the high rock above the worlds'.
segments.s251-g14: بدان here functions as 'to that' (poetic compound of به + آن) introducing the destination/target; چاه is 'well/pit'; the following coordinated adjectives اندوه و گرم و گداز ('sorrow and heat and moltenness') describe the pit. The sequence is a compressed poetic description: 'toward that pit of sorrow, heat, and melting.'
segments.s251-g15: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; گفت is the verb 'said/spoke' (here used with با + addressee), but the provided sense list for گفت does not include the verbal 'to say' so a direct match is not listed; با + نامور هفت گرد means 'to/with the famed Haft-Gird' — نامور modifies the name (postpositive fame epithet). In context this reads 'thus he spoke to the famed Haft-Gird.'
segments.s251-g16: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that'; روی زمین را is the definite object 'the surface/face of the earth' (روی 'surface' modifying زمین, with را marking the definite object); بباید is the modal 'must/ought' (classical form), and سترد is the verb 'to cover/veil' (here in its plain form following the modal). The clause = 'that he must cover the face of the earth.'
segments.s251-g49: This is a short reported-speech clause. بدو is the prepositional/adverbial 'to him' (classical be + pronoun), گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used as a reporting verb), and بیژن is the speaker's name (subject). که‌ای یار من follows as a vocative: که‌ای is an archaic/exclamatory address equivalent to 'O', یار 'companion/friend', and من 'my' (possessive after the noun).
segments.s251-g50: ندانی is the second-person present negative question form (ن- negative prefix + دانی 'you know'): 'do you not know'. که introduces the subordinate interrogative clause. چون functions as an interrogative 'how' here, بود is the past copula 'was/went', and پیکار من is the noun phrase 'my battle' (پیکار 'battle', من 'my'). The clause therefore asks 'do you not know how my battle was/went?'.
segments.s251-g51: Another direct address/question: ندانی again is 'do you not know' (2nd-person negative present). تو‌ای is the emphatic/vocative 'you' (classical poetic 'thou/O you'), مهتر is an honorific 'chief/great one', and شیرمرد is an epithet 'brave man/champion' — together an elevated form of address 'O great champion'.
segments.s251-g52: که introduces the content clause 'that/what ...'. گرگین is the proper name Gorgin here, and میلاد is another name (rendered in the senses list as 'birth' but used as a personal name in the narrative). با من is the prepositional phrase 'to/with me' (با 'with/to', من 'me/my'), چه is the interrogative 'what', and کرد is the past-tense verb 'did'. Together: 'what Gorgin (and) Milad did to me'.
segments.s251-g53: گرافتد is an archaic/past-form verb meaning 'was seized' or 'was taken' (here passive sense). بروبر is an adverbial compound meaning 'face to face' / 'right in front', and جهانبین is an epithet or nickname ('world-seer') used here as a kin/possessive phrase with من 'my' following it. The phrase describes the seizure occurring 'right before my Jahān‑bīn'.
segments.s251-g54: This clause states a consequence. برو here is an archaic/poetic particle used to introduce or intensify the following noun (literally 'go' in modern Persian but here part of the formulaic phrase), رستخیز means 'resurrection' or 'uprising', آید is the present/future-tense verb 'will come/comes', از is the preposition 'from', کین is 'vengeance/hatred', and من 'my' marks possession. The sense: 'then/from that an uprising will come from my vengeance.'
segments.s251-g55: بدو گفت رستم is the reporting phrase 'Rostam said to him' (بدو 'to him', گفت 'said', رستم subject). که‌گر is the conditional particle 'if' (archaic compound of که + گر), and بدخوی is an adjective 'ill‑tempered/ill‑humoured' describing the addressee — introducing the 'if' condition.
segments.s251-g56: بیاری here stands for being without or refusing help (in the manuscript tradition it functions as 'help/support' or 'taking help'); و is the conjunction 'and', گفتار من is 'my words/speech' (گفتار 'speech', من 'my'), and نشنوی is the second-person present negative 'you do not hear/do not heed' (ن- + شنوی). The clause lists the things the addressee does (or refuses): 'refuse help and do not listen to my words.'
segments.s251-g17: بباید is a modal predicate meaning 'must/ought' and governs an infinitive clause; شما را is the second-person object/experiencer marked with the enclitic را (here indicating who is obliged); کنون is the temporal adverb 'now' modifying the action; ساختن is the verbal infinitive 'to build/make' serving as the complement of بباید.
segments.s251-g18: سر چاه is a noun phrase where سر means 'top/mouth' (of the well); از سنگ is a material phrase 'out of/from stone' (از marks material here); پرداختن is an infinitive used here in the sense 'to finish/face/lay' (i.e. to make or finish the stonework). Together this gives 'to make/finish the well's head out of stone.'
segments.s251-g19: پیاده شدند is a past plural verbal phrase: پیاده 'on foot' with شدِند as the past auxiliary meaning 'they became' → here 'they dismounted/went on foot'; آن سران سپاه is a noun phrase 'those leaders of the army' (آن = those/the, سران = leaders, سپاه = army) and is the subject of شدند.
segments.s251-g20: کزان is a relative/adverbial 'from which' or 'therefrom' linking to the place just mentioned; سنگ پردخت is a nominal compound where پردخت (here a noun) means 'stone-finish/stonework'; مانند چاه is a simile 'like a well'. The clause explains why the leaders dismounted: the stonework resembled a well.
segments.s251-g21: بسودند is a past-plural verb here used in a poetic/archaic sense 'they rushed/advanced/leapt' (classical usage); بسیار is an adverb 'greatly/many' modifying the verb; بر is the preposition 'onto/upon' and سنگ چنگ is a noun phrase 'the rock (سنگ) with handholds or claws (چنگ)' — i.e. the rock they leapt upon.
segments.s251-g22: شده مانده is a compound perfect-like sequence: شده (past participle) + مانده ('remained') yielding 'had remained/was left'; گردان is the noun 'troop' (subject that remained); و links clauses; آسوده سنگ is a verbless nominal phrase in which آسوده (at peace/settled) qualifies سنگ, producing 'and the stone was at rest' (adjectival predicate implied).
segments.s251-g23: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; از نامداران is a partitive/agentive phrase 'one of the famed/renowned' (از marks 'of'); بپالود is a past-form verb 'he purified/controlled' (ب‑ prefix + فعل) and takes خوی ('disposition, temper') as its object — together 'when a famed man checked or composed his temper'.
segments.s251-g24: که introduces the subordinate clause 'so that/that'; سنگ از سر چاه is the noun phrase 'the stone from the top (سر = 'top, rim') of the well'; ننهاد is the negative past form of نهاد 'to place/set' (here 'did not set/move/let fall'); پی is an adverb 'after/any further' (here 'afterwards' or 'in consequence'). The clause thus states that the stone did not move/fall from the well's lip afterward.
segments.s251-g41: بکنده is a past-participial/perfective form meaning 'torn away' or 'uprooted' and appears before the patient. دلم is the noun 'دل' with the enclitic -م 'my' (so 'my heart'); زین سرای سپنج is a prepositional phrase 'from this house/abode (سرای) of sponge (سپنج)' giving the source. Word order is verb/participle → patient → source.
segments.s251-g42: ز introduces the cause/source 'from/because of'. بس here has the force 'so much' / 'great amount' (classical use). The following nouns are a piled list of afflictions ('pain, hardship, sorrow, and suffering') joined by و; this whole phrase gives the reason for the preceding verbal action.
segments.s251-g43: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' and precedes گفت 'said' (here an action of saying by رستم, the subject that follows). که introduces a subordinate clause. بر جان تو is a prepositional phrase 'upon your life' (= 'your life' as the thing affected).
segments.s251-g44: ببخشود is a 3rd-person past-tense verb meaning 'he spared/forgave/granted' and is the main verb of the subordinate clause. روشن modifies جهانبان ('the bright/world‑guardian') and تو is the possessive 'your' attached after the noun in this poetic order. The whole segment states who spared the addressee's life.
segments.s251-g45: کنون‌ای is the vocative adverbial 'now, O …' (کنون + vocative ای). خردمند and آزاده are adjectives modifying the vocative addressee; خوی here is a noun meaning 'disposition' or part of the compound conveying 'noble‑natured'—all together form the address 'O wise, noble‑natured one.'
segments.s251-g46: مرا هست is an existential construction meaning 'there is for me' → 'I have'; با تو 'with you' marks whose attention/request is intended; یکی آرزوی is the object 'one wish' or 'one request'. Word order places the existential verb before the quantified object.
segments.s251-g47: بمن بخش is an imperative phrase 'give to me' (بمن = 'to me', بخش = 'give/allocate'); the object is marked by را and consists of the names/words گرگین میلاد treated here as the thing demanded (likely proper names). The particle را marks the definite object. (Note: گرگین and میلاد here function as names/identifiers.)
segments.s251-g48: ز دل 'from the heart' is the source phrase; دور کن is the imperative 'make distant / drive away'. کین و بیداد را is the definite object (hatred and injustice) marked by را. Together: 'Drive hatred and injustice from (my) heart.'
segments.s251-g33: همه نوش بودی is a compact past statement: همه نوش (a compound noun phrase 'all/whole delight') is the predicate noun and بودی is the 2nd-person past of بودن ('you were'). ز + گیتیت بهر is a short prepositional/benefactive phrase attached after the predicate; ز 'from/of' + گیتیت 'the world' (here 'world' in a poetical sense) and بهر 'for/for the sake of' gives a flavor of 'the delight of the world' or 'the world's delight'. The subject 'تو' (you) is omitted and understood from بودی (common in Persian).
segments.s251-g34: ز دستش چرا بستدی جام زهر is a question built around the verb بستدی (past 'closed/fastened' or 'caused to be sealed/affixed'); ز دستش ('from/by his hand') is an ablative/agent phrase indicating the instrument or source; چرا is 'why' and introduces the question; جام زهر 'cup of poison' is the object. Word order is typical older/poetic Persian: circumstantial phrase (ز دستش) + question word (چرا) + verb (بستدی) + object (جام زهر).
segments.s251-g35: بدو گفت بیژن ز تاریک چاه has the indirect-object/allative pronoun بدو ('to him') fronted for emphasis, followed by گفت (here the past verb 'said') and then the subject بیژن (Bijan) — verb–subject order is common in classical Persian. ز تاریک چاه is an ablative/locative phrase 'from the dark pit' giving Bijan's location/source when he spoke.
segments.s251-g36: که introduces a subordinate (content) clause here; چون functions as an interrogative/relativizer meaning 'how' or 'in what way' and بود is the past of بودن ('was'/'befell'). بر پهلوان is a prepositional phrase 'upon the hero' and رنج راه ('the hardship of the road') is the theme/subject of that predicate: literal order 'that how was upon the hero the hardship of the way.'
segments.s251-g37: مرا چون خروش تو آمد بگوش is a temporal clause: مرا (me) is fronted (object-marking is split here in the manuscript into م + را), چون is the temporal/conditional 'when', خروش تو 'your cry' is the subject, آمد is the past verb 'came', and بگوش is an adverbial phrase 'to (my) ear' or 'to the hearing'. The order places the affected person (مرا) at the start for emphasis.
segments.s251-g38: همه زهر گیتی مرا گشت نوش is a result clause: همه زهر گیتی 'all the world's poison' is the grammatical subject/agent, مرا (again split into particles) is the affected object, گشت is the past copular/transformative verb 'became/turned', and نوش is the predicative noun 'delight' (i.e. 'became delight'). The structure is Subject – [object marked] – verb – predicative noun, a common poetic order.
segments.s251-g39: بدین سان که بینی مرا خان و مان begins with the fixed classical phrase بدین سان 'in this way/manner' (here shown in manuscript-split pieces), که introduces the resulting clause, بینی is present 2nd-person singular 'you see', مرا is the object 'me' (again split into particles in the tokenization), and خان و مان is a paired noun phrase 'khan and man' (an archaic/poetic pairing meaning lord and commoner or 'high and low' — here describing the speaker's perceived status). The clause reads 'that you see me khan and man.'
segments.s251-g40: ز آهن زمین و ز سنگ آسمان is a pair of parallel prepositional phrases (ز + material + possessor) forming a vivid simile: 'from the iron of the earth and from the stone of the sky.' Each ز marks source/material (ablative), آهن/سنگ name the materials (iron, stone), and زمین/آسمان give the poetic loci (earth, sky). The parallelism is typical of Ferdowsi's elevated style.
segments.s251-g57: بمانم is a 1st‑person present/subjunctive form (literally “I remain” or a volitional “I will leave/keep”). ترا is split into the enclitic second‑person object -ت and the object marker را (both attached after the verb here, a common older/poetic order). بسته is a past participle/adjective “bound/tied” describing that object. در چاه پای is an inverted locative phrase: پای = “foot/base (of)” + در چاه = “in the well,” so together “at the foot/bottom of the well.”
segments.s251-g58: برخش appears as a terse/poetic verb form (older orthography and/or contraction; context shows it functions as an action placed before the following clause). اندر is the locative “in/within.” آرم شوم is a two‑verb sequence where آرم is a 1st‑person present/subjunctive “I rest” and شوم is a jussive/optative marker “may I become / I shall be,” producing the sense “so that I may be at rest” or “and I shall be at ease.” باز is the adverb “again,” and جای is the noun “place” — together indicating a return “back to (my) place.” The whole segment expresses an action (put/leave) followed by the speaker’s intent to be at rest and return.
segments.s251-g59: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” گفتار is the noun “speech/words.” رستم is the proper name (Rostam). رسیدش is the past verb رسید with the enclitic -ش “to him” (so “reached him” or “reached his ear”). بگوش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase “to the ear / into his hearing.” Word order places the temporal clause first and the verbal phrase at the end.
segments.s251-g60: ازان is a compound of از + آن, “from that / from it.” تنگ زندان is an adjectival + noun sequence where تنگ “narrow” modifies زندان “prison/dungeon” (word order poetic: adjective before noun). برآمد is the past verb “arose/issued forth.” خروش is the noun “cry/roar” and here functions as the subject of برآمد, yielding the idiomatic inverted order “a cry arose.”
segments.s251-g61: چنین داد پاسخ is a conventional formula: چنین “thus/so” precedes داد پاسخ “gave an answer” (داد is the past tense ‘gave’). که introduces the reported speech/complement clause. بد بخت من is the predicative phrase “ill‑fated/unfortunate me” (بد modifies بخت to make the compound “بدبخت” = unlucky), with من as the 1st‑person pronoun.
segments.s251-g62: ز here is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of.’ گردان is a noun meaning ‘troop/host’ (so ‘from the troop’ / ‘on account of the army’). وز is an older/poetic particle (here best read as ‘from/through the blast/force of’ in combination with the following noun). دوده is ‘smoke’ and و is the normal conjunction ‘and,’ while انجمن is ‘assembly/gathering/crowd.’ Together the phrase lists causes of his misfortune: the troop, the smoke (blast), and the crowd.
segments.s251-g63: ز is the preposition “from.” گرگین is the proper name Gorgin (here the agent/source of the misfortune). بـ (the short prefix be‑) plus دان in this poetic spacing corresponds to the older contracted بدَان / به آن meaning ‘to/of that’ (a poetic contraction equivalent to modern به آن); بد is the adjective ‘bad/evil’ referring back to that misfortune. که introduces the relative clause ‘which,’ and بر من رسید is the verbal phrase ‘befell me’ (بر = upon, من = me, رسید = reached).
segments.s251-g64: چنین روز is ‘such a day / a day like this.’ نیـزم is the adverb نیز “also” with the clitic -م ‘to/for me’ (so ‘for me also’). بباید is the modal verb ‘must/ought’ (present/future sense), and کشید is the past/root form of کشیدن used here in the sense ‘to suffer/endure’ (poetic usage: ‘must be borne’). The clause means ‘I too must endure such a day.’

sentences.s251-s33: We endured and became pleased with him; yet my heart found no rest from its hatred of him.
sentences.s251-s34: Rostam cast (or scattered) the snare into the prison; he drew him out of the well with his feet bound.
sentences.s251-s35: His body was naked, his hair and nails long, consumed by suffering, pain, and longing.
sentences.s251-s36: His whole body was full of blood and his complexion pale; the chain of that fetter had become rusted.
sentences.s251-s1: At that moment when Rostam loosened the Berber's knot and struck the knot upon the armor.
sentences.s251-s2: The sun and the moon came before (appeared before) God; they came to him and became his support and refuge.
sentences.s251-s3: He kept saying, 'May that eye be blind!' — 'May Bijan be my strength in this matter.'
sentences.s251-s4: He commanded 'Beger-dān' so that likewise they bound a fetter of hatred upon the place.
sentences.s251-s41: After that, when Gorgin came near him, he approached and rubbed his face on the ground.
sentences.s251-s42: For that bad deed he made an apology before him, and he turned away from that rash action of his.
sentences.s251-s43: Bijan's heart, stirred by his hatred, set out on the road; he did not fail to exact retribution for the offense.
sentences.s251-s44: They loaded the camels and saddled the horses; Rostam put on his chosen arms.
sentences.s251-s45: They took their places among the renowned, and drew sword and heavy mace.
sentences.s251-s46: Someone loaded the baggage and put the affairs in order, just as was fitting for the battlefield.
sentences.s251-s47: Then he rose with Ashkash's pack — the quick‑witted one — who keeps the army alert everywhere.
sentences.s251-s48: Rostam commanded Bijan: 'You go with Ashkash and with Manijeh.'
sentences.s251-s49: for tonight, because of Afrasiab's hatred, we will find neither rest nor food nor sleep.
sentences.s251-s50: I will contrive a plan now at his gate so that tomorrow he will laugh and go to his country.
sentences.s251-s51: Bijan said to him, 'I am the vanguard, for they will make enmity against me anew.'
sentences.s251-s37: Rostam cried out when he saw him; the whole body turned to iron and vanished.
sentences.s251-s38: He struck with his hand and the chain and bond broke; he freed from it the ring of the foot-fetter.
sentences.s251-s39: They went toward the house from that well-field; Bijan (supported) by one hand, the others in tatters.
sentences.s251-s40: Tahmētan ordered that his head be washed; someone put a new garment on him.
sentences.s251-s13: From Rakhsh there came forth, as if a lion; he struck the hem of his armor about his waist.
sentences.s251-s14: He sought strength from God, the life-giver; he struck with his hand and lifted up that stone, the scythe.
sentences.s251-s15: He hurled it into the grove near the city of Chin; the earth trembled from that stone.
sentences.s251-s16: He asked Bijan and wailed bitterly, 'How did your affair come to such ill fate?'
sentences.s251-s5: They put saddles and daggers on the horses and sharpened every weapon's claw for the fight.
sentences.s251-s6: Tahmētan, radiant, set his face and went forward along the inner path of the stream.
sentences.s251-s7: When he came upon the high stone above the worlds, toward that pit of sorrow, heat, and moltenness.
sentences.s251-s8: Thus he spoke to the famed Haft-Gird that he must cover the surface of the earth.
sentences.s251-s25: Bijan said to him, "O my friend, do you not know how my battle went?"
sentences.s251-s26: Do you not know, O great champion, what Gorgin and Milad did to me?
sentences.s251-s27: He was seized right before my Jahān‑bīn; then from my vengeance an uprising will come.
sentences.s251-s28: Rostam said to him, 'If you are ill‑humoured, refuse help and do not heed my words.'
sentences.s251-s9: Now you must make (build) the top (mouth) of the well out of stone.
sentences.s251-s10: Those leaders of the army dismounted, for the stonework there looked like a well.
sentences.s251-s11: They rushed (leapt) in great numbers onto the stone; the troop remained, and the stone lay at rest.
sentences.s251-s12: When one of the renowned men controlled/composed his disposition, the stone did not move (fall) from the lip of the well afterwards.
sentences.s251-s21: My heart has been torn away from this sponge-like dwelling by so much pain, hardship, sorrow, and suffering.
sentences.s251-s22: Rostam said to him that the bright world‑guardian had spared your life.
sentences.s251-s23: Now, O wise and noble-hearted one, I have one request of you.
sentences.s251-s24: Give me Gorgīn Milād; drive hatred and injustice from my heart.
sentences.s251-s17: You were the world's entire delight — why then did you, from his hand, fasten (seal/offer) the cup of poison?
sentences.s251-s18: Bijan said to him from the dark pit how the hero fared on the suffering road.
sentences.s251-s19: When your cry came to my ear, all the world's poison turned into a delight for me.
sentences.s251-s20: In this manner — that you see me both khan and man, of the earth's iron and of the sky's stone.
sentences.s251-s29: I'll leave you bound at the bottom of the well; I'll set him in it and then return to my place.
sentences.s251-s30: When Rostam’s words reached his ear, from that narrow prison a cry arose.
sentences.s251-s31: He answered thus: “Unlucky am I, because of the troops and from the smoke and the gathering.”
sentences.s251-s32: From Gorgin came that ill which befell me; such a day I too must endure.
